title: SEGMENT 18 — The frontier: universal donors, chimeras, and the road to routine

lines.text: SEGMENT 18 — The frontier: universal donors, chimeras, and the road to routine
Let me close by sketching where the field is going.
The trajectory, if you extrapolate from where we are now, points in several directions at once.
The first direction is further multi-edit pigs.
Both eGenesis and Revivicor are working on pigs with even more edits — knocking out additional pig-specific MHC genes, adding additional human tolerance-inducing transgenes.

The goal is what is sometimes called a universal donor pig — an animal whose organs can be transplanted into any human recipient with minimal immune suppression, regardless of the recipient's antibody profile.
We are not there yet, but the trajectory is toward fewer drugs and less rejection per generation of pig.
The second direction is in-pig humanization — the blastocyst complementation work I mentioned in the last segment.
If you can grow a fully human pancreas inside a pig, you have eliminated the antigen problem entirely.
Several groups are pursuing this.
The current bottleneck is the developmental biology — how do you get enough human cells to contribute to the relevant organ, in the relevant proportion, in the relevant developmental window.
Some labs have demonstrated pig embryos with substantial human cellular contribution.
None have yet produced a transplantable humanized organ.
The path is long but the destination is real.
The third direction is routine clinical xeno.
The Phase 1 trials that begin in 2025, if successful, will be followed by Phase 2 trials, then Phase 3, then first approvals around 2027 to 2028, then expanded clinical access.

By the early 2030s, if all goes well, a patient on the kidney waiting list might be offered, as a standard of care, either a human kidney or an edited pig kidney, depending on availability and compatibility.
By the late 2030s, the human waiting list may begin to shrink, not because more people are donating, but because the supply of pig kidneys is keeping up with demand.
Will it actually go that way?
I want to be honest.
The field has a habit of overpromising and underdelivering.
Every five years from 1990 to 2015, somebody announced that clinical xeno was just around the corner, and it wasn't.
The current optimism could turn out to be premature again.
There could be infectious disease setbacks.
There could be long-term immune issues that don't manifest in the first year of patient follow-up.
There could be regulatory delays.
There could be public opinion shifts that constrain the field.
But the underlying technology is real in a way it wasn't before.
CRISPR was the enabling technology.
Without programmable, multiplexed gene editing, you cannot make a 69-edit pig, you cannot inactivate the PERVs, you cannot stack the human transgenes.
The 2020s are the first decade in which the genetic toolkit was, finally, sufficient to address the immune barriers that had blocked the field for fifty years.
From impossible in 2000 to clinical reality in 2024 — that trajectory is unlikely to reverse.
If I had to bet, I would say: by the end of this decade, there will be at least a few hundred patients living with xenografted organs, mostly kidneys.
By the end of next decade, the technology will have moved from experimental to standard of care, at least for the highest-need patients.
The seventeen-people-a-day on the waiting list will begin to be served by a supply chain that runs through Virginia and Massachusetts barrier facilities, not just through brain-death-trauma donors.
That is the world that xenotransplantation is, finally, beginning to build.
We started two hours ago with one sentence.
Xenotransplantation is borrowing an organ from a pig — and CRISPR is what finally let us do it.
I hope, having now spent two hours on it, that you can do the three things I asked you for.
Explain why alpha-gal was the central villain for fifty years.
Walk through the modern multi-edit pig, gene by gene.
Tell the story of Bennett, Slayman, Pisano, and Looney, and what each one taught the field.
If you can do those three things, you understand xenotransplantation as well as anyone outside of the field.
The rest is updates — new patients, new trials, new edits.
But the framework is what we've built today.
The field is yours to follow.
Thank you.
We'll take questions.
